events.description: Generates a complete recipe with ingredients, technique guidance, and nutritional breakdown

code: var request = await ask(
await stop(request)
var cuisine = loadKnowledge({ "cooking": { cuisine: { type: { italian: true } } } })
var dietary = loadKnowledge({ "cooking": { dietary: { restriction: { vegetarian: true } } } })
var technique = loadKnowledge({ "cooking": { technique: { method: { saute: true, braise: true, grill: true } } } })
await stop(cuisine, dietary, technique)

cuisine.display:
{ "cooking": { "cuisine": { "type": { "italian": "# Italian Cuisine

Italian cooking centers on high-quality, seasonal ingredients prepared simply to let flavors shine.

## Key Principles
- **Simplicity** — Most dishes use 4–8 ingredients. The quality of each ingredient matters more than complexity.
- **Regionality** — Northern Italy favors butter, rice, and polenta; Southern Italy uses olive oil, tomatoes, and dried pasta.
- **Al dente** — Pasta should be cooked firm to the bite. Start tasting 2 minutes before the package time.
- **Soffritto** — The holy trinity of onion, carrot, and celery, slowly sweated in olive oil, forms the base of countless dishes.

## Essential Techniques
- **Emulsifying pasta water** — Reserve starchy pasta water and toss with sauce to create a silky coating.
- **Risotto stirring** — Constant stirring releases starch for a creamy texture without cream.
- **Bruschetta method** — Rub toasted bread with raw garlic, then drizzle with good olive oil.

## Staple Ingredients
Olive oil, San Marzano tomatoes, Parmigiano-Reggiano, fresh basil, garlic, dried pasta, arborio rice, prosciutto, mozzarella.

## Classic Dishes
Cacio e pepe, carbonara, margherita pizza, risotto alla milanese, osso buco, tiramisu." } } } }

dietary.display:
{ "cooking": { "dietary": { "restriction": { "vegetarian": "# Vegetarian Cooking

Vegetarian cooking excludes meat and fish but includes dairy, eggs, and honey.

## Substitution Strategies
- **Protein sources** — Eggs, cheese, legumes (lentils, chickpeas, black beans), tofu, tempeh, seitan, nuts
- **Umami replacements** — Soy sauce, miso paste, nutritional yeast, aged cheeses, mushrooms, tomato paste
- **Stock** — Use vegetable stock or mushroom stock instead of chicken/beef stock
- **Gelatin** — Replace with agar-agar (1:1 ratio, but boil to activate)

## Tips
- Roast vegetables at high heat (220°C+) for caramelization and depth
- Use smoked paprika, liquid smoke, or charred vegetables for smoky notes usually provided by meat
- Don't treat vegetarian food as "meat minus the meat" — build dishes around vegetables as the star" } } } }

technique.display:
{ "cooking": { "technique": { "method": { "saute": "# Sauté

High-heat, quick cooking in a wide pan with a small amount of fat. The word comes from French "sauter" — to jump.

## When to Use
- Vegetables that should stay crisp-tender (bell peppers, snap peas, asparagus)
- Thin cuts of meat or fish (chicken cutlets, shrimp, scallops)
- Finishing a dish with a pan sauce after searing

## Key Technique
1. **Heat the pan first**, then add oil. The oil should shimmer but not smoke.
2. **Don't overcrowd** — ingredients should sit in a single layer with space between them. Work in batches if needed.
3. **Keep things moving** — toss or stir frequently. The goal is even browning without burning.
4. **Season at the end** — salt draws out moisture and can prevent browning.

## Equipment
A wide, flat-bottomed skillet or sauté pan. Stainless steel gives the best fond (browned bits) for pan sauces. Non-stick works for delicate items like fish.

## Common Mistakes
- Starting with a cold pan (food steams instead of searing)
- Adding too much food at once (temperature drops, food boils)
- Moving food too often when you want a sear (let it sit for 2–3 minutes)", "braise": "# Braise

Braising combines dry heat (searing) and wet heat (simmering in liquid) for dishes that are deeply flavorful and fall-apart tender.

## When to Use
- Tough, collagen-rich cuts of meat (short ribs, pork shoulder, lamb shanks, chuck roast)
- Root vegetables that benefit from long cooking (carrots, turnips, parsnips)
- Any dish where you want rich, concentrated sauce built from the cooking liquid

## Key Technique
1. **Sear first** — Pat the protein dry, season generously, and sear in hot oil until deeply browned on all sides. This is where most of the flavor comes from.
2. **Build aromatics** — Sauté onions, carrots, celery, and garlic in the same pot after searing.
3. **Deglaze** — Add wine, stock, or other liquid and scrape up the fond (browned bits).
4. **Submerge halfway** — The liquid should come about halfway up the protein, not cover it completely.
5. **Low and slow** — Cover and cook at 150–165°C (300–325°F) for 2–4 hours until fork-tender.
6. **Rest and reduce** — Remove the protein, strain and reduce the braising liquid into a sauce.

## Equipment
A heavy Dutch oven with a tight-fitting lid. Cast iron or enameled cast iron retains heat evenly.

## Common Mistakes
- Skipping the sear (you lose enormous flavor)
- Cooking at too high a temperature (the protein toughens before the collagen breaks down)
- Not reducing the braising liquid (thin, watery sauce instead of a rich glaze)", "grill": "# Grill

Grilling uses direct radiant heat from below to cook food quickly, creating characteristic char marks and smoky flavor.

## When to Use
- Steaks, burgers, chops, and other quick-cooking proteins
- Vegetables with enough structure to hold together (corn, zucchini, peppers, eggplant)
- Flatbreads, pizza, and fruit (peaches, pineapple)

## Key Technique
1. **Preheat thoroughly** — 15–20 minutes with the lid closed. The grates should be too hot to hold your hand near for more than 2 seconds.
2. **Clean and oil the grates** — Brush with a grill brush, then oil with a folded paper towel dipped in high-smoke-point oil.
3. **Two-zone setup** — Pile coals on one side (or turn on only half the burners) for a hot sear zone and a cooler indirect zone.
4. **Don't press** — Pressing burgers or chicken squeezes out juices. Let the food sit until it releases naturally.
5. **Rest the meat** — Rest for 5–10 minutes after grilling. Internal temperature will rise 3–5°F during resting.

## Temperature Guide
- **High (230°C+ / 450°F+)** — Steaks, thin cuts, vegetables
- **Medium (175–230°C / 350–450°F)** — Chicken pieces, fish, sausages
- **Low indirect (120–150°C / 250–300°F)** — Whole chickens, ribs, large roasts

## Common Mistakes
- Opening the lid too often (loses heat and extends cooking time)
- Only using direct heat (thick cuts burn outside before cooking through)
- Not letting the grill preheat (food sticks and doesn't char properly)" } } } }

code:
completeTask("recipe", "select-cuisine-and-dish", {
  cuisineType: "italian",
  dishName: "Pasta Primavera",
  description: "A classic Italian pasta dish showcasing seasonal vegetables in a light olive oil and garlic sauce",
  servings: 4,
  estimatedTime: "30 minutes",
  difficulty: "Easy",
  rationale: "Perfect for showcasing Italian simplicity with seasonal vegetables, vegetarian-friendly, and uses sauté technique"
})
// Create recipe ingredients
completeTask("recipe", "gather-ingredients", ingredients)
// Create mise en place

var mise = {
  advancePrep: [
    "Wash and dice all vegetables",
    "Chop fresh herbs",
    "Grate cheese",
    "Bring large pot of salted water to boil"
  ],
  immediatePrep: [
    "Cook pasta according to package directions until al dente",
    "While pasta cooks, heat olive oil in large skillet",
    "Reserve 1/2 cup pasta water before draining"
  ],
  equipmentNeeded: [
    "Large pot (6-8qt) for pasta",
    "Large skillet or sauté pan",
    "Colander",
    "Tongs or spider strainer",
    "Cutting board and knife",
    "Measuring cups"
  ]
}
completeTask("recipe", "prepare-mise-en-place", mise)
// Create cooking steps

var cookingSteps = {
  steps: [
    "Bring a large pot of generously salted water to boil. Add pasta and cook until al dente (start tasting 2 minutes before package time)",
    "While pasta cooks, heat 1/3 cup olive oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat",
    "Add diced zucchini and bell pepper to the hot oil. Sauté for 3-4 minutes until vegetables begin to soften",
    "Add minced garlic and sauté for 30 seconds until fragrant (don't let it brown)",
    "Add cherry tomatoes and peas. Continue sautéing for 2-3 minutes until tomatoes soften",
    "Reserve 1/2 cup pasta cooking water, then drain pasta",
    "Add drained pasta to the skillet with vegetables. Toss everything together",
    "Add sun-dried tomatoes, fresh basil, and parsley. Mix well",
    "Add reserved pasta water a little at a time, tossing to create a light sauce that coats the pasta",
    "Season with salt and pepper to taste",
    "Remove from heat and stir in half the grated Parmigiano-Reggiano",
    "Divide among 4 plates and top with remaining cheese. Serve immediately"
  ],
  totalActiveTime: "20 minutes",
  totalPassiveTime: "10 minutes"
}
completeTask("recipe", "cook-step-by-step", cookingSteps)
// Spawn nutrition agent for analysis
// Create a simple nutrition analysis using the cooking knowledge

var nutritionInfo = {
  perServing: {
    calories: "Approximately 450-500 kcal",
    protein: "18g",
    carbohydrates: "65g (complex carbs from pasta and vegetables)",
    fat: "18g (mostly heart-healthy monounsaturated from olive oil)",
    fiber: "8g",
    sodium: "450mg (adjust based on salt usage)"
  },
  nutrientSources: [
    "Pasta provides complex carbohydrates for sustained energy",
    "Vegetables offer fiber, vitamins A and C, and antioxidants",
    "Olive oil provides healthy monounsaturated fats",
    "Parmigiano-Reggiano adds protein and calcium",
    "Herbs contribute micronutrients and fresh flavors"
  ],
  dietaryNotes: [
    "Vegetarian-friendly recipe with no meat products",
    "Rich in fiber from vegetables and whole grain pasta",
    "Olive oil provides heart-healthy monounsaturated fats",
    "Contains moderate sodium levels (can be reduced by using less cheese)",
    "Good balance of complex carbs, vegetables, and healthy fats"
  ]
}
completeTask("recipe", "analyze-nutrition", nutritionInfo)
// Display the complete recipe

display(<RecipeCard
  name="Pasta Primavera"
  cuisine="Italian"
  method="Sauté"
  servings={4}
  time="30 minutes"
  ingredients={[
    "400g dried spaghetti or fettuccine",
    "2 medium zucchini, diced",
    "1 red bell pepper, diced",
    "1 cup cherry tomatoes, halved",
    "1 cup fresh peas (or frozen, thawed)",
    "1/2 cup sun-dried tomatoes in oil, chopped",
    "3 cloves garlic, minced",
    "1/3 cup extra virgin olive oil",
    "1/4 cup fresh basil, chopped",
    "1/4 cup fresh parsley, chopped",
    "Salt and freshly ground black pepper to taste",
    "1/2 cup grated Parmigiano-Reggiano cheese",
    "1/2 cup reserved pasta water"
  ]}
  steps={[
    "Bring a large pot of generously salted water to boil. Add pasta and cook until al dente",
    "Heat olive oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat",
    "Sauté zucchini and bell pepper for 3-4 minutes until softened",
    "Add garlic and sauté 30 seconds until fragrant",
    "Add cherry tomatoes and peas, sauté 2-3 minutes until tomatoes soften",
    "Reserve 1/2 cup pasta water, then drain pasta",
    "Add pasta to skillet with vegetables and toss",
    "Add sun-dried tomatoes, basil, and parsley, mix well",
    "Add pasta water gradually to create a light sauce",
    "Season with salt and pepper, remove from heat",
    "Stir in half the Parmigiano-Reggiano",
    "Divide among 4 plates and top with remaining cheese. Serve immediately"
  ]}
/>)

plan.description: Generates a complete recipe with ingredients, technique guidance, and nutritional breakdown

output.description: A classic Italian pasta dish showcasing seasonal vegetables in a light olive oil and garlic sauce
output.rationale: Perfect for showcasing Italian simplicity with seasonal vegetables, vegetarian-friendly, and uses sauté technique

categories.name: Vegetables
items: 1/2 cup sun-dried tomatoes in oil, chopped

categories.name: Sauce & Seasoning
items: Salt and freshly ground black pepper to taste

advancePrep: Bring large pot of salted water to boil
immediatePrep: Cook pasta according to package directions until al dente
While pasta cooks, heat olive oil in large skillet
Reserve 1/2 cup pasta water before draining

steps: Bring a large pot of generously salted water to boil. Add pasta and cook until al dente (start tasting 2 minutes before package time)
While pasta cooks, heat 1/3 cup olive oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat
Add diced zucchini and bell pepper to the hot oil. Sauté for 3-4 minutes until vegetables begin to soften
Add minced garlic and sauté for 30 seconds until fragrant (don't let it brown)
Add cherry tomatoes and peas. Continue sautéing for 2-3 minutes until tomatoes soften
Reserve 1/2 cup pasta cooking water, then drain pasta
Add drained pasta to the skillet with vegetables. Toss everything together
Add sun-dried tomatoes, fresh basil, and parsley. Mix well
Add reserved pasta water a little at a time, tossing to create a light sauce that coats the pasta
Season with salt and pepper to taste
Remove from heat and stir in half the grated Parmigiano-Reggiano
Divide among 4 plates and top with remaining cheese. Serve immediately

nutrientSources: Pasta provides complex carbohydrates for sustained energy
Vegetables offer fiber, vitamins A and C, and antioxidants
Olive oil provides healthy monounsaturated fats
Herbs contribute micronutrients and fresh flavors
dietaryNotes: Vegetarian-friendly recipe with no meat products
Rich in fiber from vegetables and whole grain pasta
Olive oil provides heart-healthy monounsaturated fats
Contains moderate sodium levels (can be reduced by using less cheese)
Good balance of complex carbs, vegetables, and healthy fats

Divide among 4 plates and top with remaining cheese. Serve immediately
